source.title: Variation in predation risk and vole feeding behaviour: a field test of the risk allocation hypothesis.

confidence_rationale: Direct field test of the canonical risk-allocation hypothesis (Lima & Bednekoff 1999) with bank voles + least weasel predator. Most predictions did NOT replicate; only the attack-ratio prediction was supported. Mixed-direction entry — important because it constrains the tolerance window for the Phase 2 falsifier and shows real-animal allocation often deviates from theoretical optima.
failure_signatures: Sundell et al's null result on most risk-allocation predictions warns against narrow tolerance windows on the Phase 2 falsifier. If ARM_1 ARC-062 produces partial-allocation (some predictions replicate, others do not), Sundell shows real animals do this too — narrow acceptance criteria would falsely reject biologically plausible behaviour.

If ARC-062 produces invariant allocation across all manipulations (no signal at all), Sundell's evidence does not provide cover — even partial-replication is a more biologically plausible signature than total non-response. A complete null is the clean falsifier signal.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Field test of the predation risk allocation hypothesis (Lima & Bednekoff 1999) using bank voles (Clethrionomys glareolus) as prey and least weasel (Mustela nivalis nivalis) as predator. Temporal pattern and intensity of predation risk were manipulated in large outdoor enclosures; foraging effort and patch use measured by giving-up densities. Most predictions of the hypothesis did NOT replicate: no variation in feeding effort due to changes in level of risk or proportion of time spent under high-risk. The only significant effect was on attack ratio — foraging effort was higher in low-attack-ratio than in high-attack-ratio treatments. Authors attribute the deviation to voles' inability to accurately assess risk-level changes, plus difficulty of testing under outdoor conditions with mammals capable of flexible behavioural patterns.
mapping.ree_translation: Important MIXED-direction empirical anchor for R4 calibration. Even when the canonical risk-allocation theory is correct in spirit (animals modulate antipredator effort by relative risk), real-animal experiments often produce only partial replication. Three implications: (1) The Phase 2 falsifier acceptance threshold should be a TOLERANCE WINDOW, not a sharp predicted ratio. Sundell shows a 5-prediction theoretical battery where 1/5 replicated empirically — the appropriate analog for ARC-062 is 'reproduces some risk-pattern dependence, not necessarily all'. (2) The single replicating prediction was attack-ratio dependence (foraging higher when attacks rare per encounter). The Phase 2 falsifier should include an attack-ratio variation as a primary metric, not just hazard-density. (3) Sundell attributes the null results partly to voles' INABILITY TO ACCURATELY ASSESS risk-level changes — a perceptual / representational limitation that maps to MECH-269 V_s monostrategy in REE. If real voles cannot represent the risk-pattern variable accurately, the architectural conclusion is that even an honest gated-policy ARC-062 may produce noisy / partial allocation responses on SD-054 if the substrate's risk signal is below the agent's representational threshold.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Bank voles in outdoor enclosures with weasel predator is one specific system; assessment failure may be species-specific. The paper does not arbitrate whether the architecture is wrong (real voles lack rule-apprehension layer adequate to the task) or the perception is wrong (voles have ARC-062-equivalent but cannot extract the risk-pattern variable from sensory input). For ARC-062 design implications, both readings are useful — the perception-failure reading is a cautionary note for SD-054's hazard signal magnitude, the architecture-failure reading is consistent with MECH-309 in another taxon.
mapping.source_context: Field test in large outdoor enclosures using bank voles (Clethrionomys glareolus) and least weasel (Mustela nivalis nivalis); manipulation of temporal risk patterns; giving-up density measurements.

confidence_components.notes: Solid Oecologia field-test paper with quantitative giving-up density measurements. Source quality reduced from highest tier because outdoor field conditions add measurement noise. Mapping fidelity moderate-high: direct test of the canonical theory, but in voles rather than fish. Transfer risk moderate: vole-to-fish behavioural-ecology transfer requires care; species differ substantially in cognitive capacity for risk assessment. The mixed-direction signal is the load-bearing finding for tolerance-window calibration.
